observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor: a. Instruct the City Accountant to prepare the list of PPE items per PPELC and compare it with the RPCPPE of the GSO; b. Direct the Property Unit to prepare a list of PPE items which are recorded in the PPELCs but not included in the RPCPPE/Physical Inventory Report as well as PPE items included in the RPCPPE but not recorded in the PPELC; c. Instruct the City Accountant and the Property Unit to submit both lists and the latest RPCPPE/Physical Inventory Report to the Inventory Committee to prepare and submit the necessary lists. These documents shall be considered as inventory working papers and shall be used by the Inventory Committee as basis in preparing the preliminary working papers as the basis for the preliminary activities of One-Time Cleansing. The Accounting Office is to draw Journal Entry Vouchers (JEVs) to reflect necessary adjustments.
observations.managementAction: The Accounting Office is currently reconciling the records of PPE in the lapsing schedule and the General Ledger. Due to the large volume of items, the process may take additional time. As of this writing, the office has separated records of PPE below the capitalization threshold for further verification and comparison with the RCPPE. The Accounting Office will coordinate closely with the CGSO and Inventory Committee to ensure compliance with MNGAS requirements for the fair presentation of PPE in the City’s financial statements and COA Circular No. 2020-006 for the one-time cleansing process. CGSO is working to reconcile discrepancies with the City Accountant’s Office and has requested a soft copy of the PPE Subsidiary Ledger as of December 31, 2024, to update reconciliation of files. Once the CY 2024 accounting records are matched with the RPCPPE, an updated reconciliation will be provided. The office has also rectified the non-compliant RPCPPE format and prepared a revised version in compliance with Appendix 49. An IIRUP has been prepared. Regarding the unserviceable motor vehicles, CGSO is awaiting technical and appraisal reports from the COA Regional Office to proceed with its disposal. Additionally, inquiries have been made regarding missing PPEs, and CGSO will continue investigations as part of the one-time cleansing process under COA Circular 2020-006.
observations.reason: Due to the large volume of items, the process may take more time.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to: a. Draw a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to recognize the interest income earned by debiting Prior Period Adjustment by P1,854,783.37; b. Present Cash in Bank - Local Currency, Time Deposits with maturity date of 360 days as Investments in the Financial Statements in the accordance with Paragraph 9 of IPSAS 2.
observations.managementAction: For the year 2024, the investment in time deposit was pre-terminated, and the proceeds, including both the principal and the interest earned, were transferred to the Local Current accounts maintained with the Land Bank of the Philippines. The Accounting Office submitted a restated financial statements in the accordance with Paragraph 9 of IPSAS 2.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor: a. Require the Accountable Officer (AO) to immediately settle her unliquidated cash advances. Henceforth, compel all employees granted with cash advance to liquidate the same as soon as the purposes for which these were given have been served; b. Instruct the City Accountant that subsequent grant of cash advance shall be supported with certification that their previous cash advances were already liquidated or proper accounting thereof is made; c. Instruct the concerned financial personnel to combine cash advances of less than P20,000.00 especially if the purpose of the cash advances is the same; d. If possible, lessen the bulk of cash advances granted to the AO and transfer them to special disbursing officers with less transactions for her to be able to liquidate her cash advances on time and update her cashbooks; and e. Install, implement and monitor a sound system of internal control to address risks and to provide reasonable assurance the operation of the agency is executed orderly, ethically, economically, efficiently and effectively, applicable laws are complied with and resources are safeguarded from loss, misuse and damage.
observations.managementAction: Instructions were made to the AO to immediately settle the unliquidated cash advances. Henceforth, compel all employees granted with cash advance to liquidate the same as soon as the purposes for which these were given have been served; Instruct the City Accountant and the Property Unit to submit both lists and the latest RPCPPE/Physical Inventory Report to the Inventory Committee to prepare and submit the necessary lists. These documents shall be considered as inventory working papers and shall be used by the Inventory Committee as basis in preparing the preliminary working papers as the basis for the preliminary activities of One-Time Cleansing. The Accounting Office will provide monthly working papers serving as subsidiary ledgers to ensure proper monitoring and accounting of CAs. Additional special disbursing officers were designated to manage disbursements.
observations.reason: The unliquidated balance at year-end includes amounts from the distribution of Assistance to Individuals in Crisis Situation (AICS) that were not claimed by the beneficiaries.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to: a. Verify the validity of payables aged two years and more and send confirmation letters for balances with identified claimants; b. Otherwise, revert to the unappropriated surplus the APs of more than two years whose claims are no longer valid; and c. Strictly comply with Section 98 of P.D. No. 1445 on the reversion of long outstanding payables.
observations.managementAction: Verifications and reconciliation of the lists/schedules of AP to the General Ledger are still ongoing. The previous practice of the City regarding unliquidated advances at the end of the year required the recognition of expenses with the corresponding credit to AP. An analysis of the Cash Disbursement Journals is still being conducted to determine the necessary adjustments to the AP.
observations.reason: Due to the volume of transactions involved, ongoing reconciliation, and the need for careful review and adjustment, the verification and reconciliation process is still ongoing.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to reconcile the difference between the accounts RPT Receivables and SET Receivables and make the necessary adjustments, furnishing the Office of the Auditor with a copy of the JEV taking into account the adjustments made.
observations.managementAction: Issues with the system related to RPT transactions were identified. The provider was notified, and the matter is currently being addressed. The City Treasurer and City Assessor established a team to review and update records through data cleansing, to provide an updated Assessment Roll and accurate records of collectibles.
observations.reason: Issues with the system related to correction of the RPT transactions were identified. The provider has been notified, and the matter is currently being addressed to ensure the accurate accounting and recording of RPT and SET.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the: a. City Treasurer to advise her disbursing officers to update the cashbook regularly and record liquidations and refund chronologically and on a timely manner in compliance with Section 28 of MNGAS for LGUs, Volume II and COA Circular No. 97-002; b. City Mayor require the City Accountant to prepare and maintain Subsidiary Ledgers for each disbursing officer pursuant to Section 10, Chapter 12 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume II; c. City Treasurer to instruct the AOs to liquidate the cash advances within the prescribed period and deny requests for additional cash advances if previous advances remain unliquidated.
observations.managementAction: The City Treasurer ensured that all CAs are liquidated within the prescribed period, with investigations conducted for any delays. Additional special disbursing officers were designated to manage disbursements, ensuring adherence to all rules and regulations. The City Accountant stated that monthly working papers, serving as subsidiary ledgers, will be provided. She also agreed that future CAs will only be granted once previous advances are fully liquidated. The monthly working papers were prepared and updated; however, the process is ongoing and not yet submitted to the Audit Team.
observations.reason: The preparation and updating of SLs is ongoing and has not yet been submitted; overlapping cash advances were granted to an AO despite non-liquidation due to inadequate monitoring and enforcement of liquidation policies.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the City Mayor: a. Direct the OIC-City GSO to process the application for insurance of all the insurable assets and properties of the City with the GSIS following the guidelines prescribed under COA Circular No. 2018-002; b. Instruct the City Budget Officer to include in the City’s annual budget the appropriation for the payment of insurance premiums of all insurable assets and properties of the City; and c. Direct the City Accountant and City Treasurer to provide necessary assistance to the OIC-GSO to ensure that all buildings and properties of the City will be covered with insurance in compliance with RA No. 656 and COA Circular No. 2018-002.
observations.managementAction: CGSO is actively working to meet the requirements of COA Circular No. 2018-002 by submitting the consolidated PIF as of December 31, 2024, which includes the latest appraised values, insurable amounts, and premiums for all insurable assets. In response to the need for insurance application, CGSO has begun coordinating with the GSIS and is in the process of finalizing the necessary documentation to ensure compliance. The City Budget Officer confirmed that insurance expenses are included in the annual budget with a 10-year summary provided; the Accounting Office confirmed coordination with the OIC-GSO to obtain insurance for other assets not yet included.
observations.reason: The ongoing completion of necessary documentation and coordination with GSIS caused the delay in implementation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer (CDRRMO), in coordination with the CDRRMC, to: a. Adequately plan and monitor the PPAs listed in the LDRRMF Investment Plan (LDRRMFIP) for effective utilization of LDRRMF; and b. Strictly comply with the provisions of Section 21 of RA No. 10121 and NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-1.
observations.managementAction: In response, the CDRRMO, in coordination with the CDRRMC, will review proposed PPAs for alignment with the LDRRMF and LDRRMF Investment Plan, prioritize feasible PPAs that address immediate risks, establish a monitoring mechanism with the Local Finance Committee, streamline the procurement process, and strengthen coordination with relevant departments and stakeholders. The City assures the Audit Team of its commitment to effective LDRRMF utilization and disaster preparedness.
observations.reason: The inadequate procurement planning for disaster preparedness and mitigation led to the low utilization of P36,181,905.64, or 37.45 percent of the P96,611,674.70 allocation, with no action taken in CY 2024 to address this issue.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to: a. See to it that disbursement vouchers prepared for the remittance of taxes covered the total amount of taxes withheld from source to avoid penalties upon payment; b. Verify the balance of P11,564,635.77 appearing under Trust Fund Book and find out if it is actually for remittance to the BIR; c. Properly monitor the taxes due for remittance to the BIR and remit in full withheld taxes to prevent imposition of interests, penalties and surcharges; d. Strictly observe pertinent provisions of Revenue Regulation No. 2-98 on withholding and remittance of taxes.
observations.managementAction: Initial analysis revealed delays in processing transactions to the Check Disbursement Journal, resulting in remittance issues, which will be monitored to ensure timely and full remittance in the future. Timing differences were identified between the recognition of 'Due to BIR' and the actual expenses and taxes withheld. Further analysis will be conducted, and any amounts withheld but not yet remitted will be promptly addressed after verification. The City Treasury identified causes of variances and addressed them as of January 31, 2025. For Trust Fund, BRS are being prepared and reconciled, and adjustments will be reflected in the books once completed.
observations.reason: Delays in processing transactions and timing differences caused the discrepancy; reconciliation and verification are ongoing.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to: a. See to it that disbursement vouchers prepared for the remittance of deductions for GSIS, PhilHealth, and Pag-IBIG matched the payroll deductions to avoid penalties; b. Verify the balances appearing under Trust Fund and find out if it is actually for remittance to GSIS, PhilHealth and Pag-IBIG; c. Strictly observe compliance with pertinent laws, rules and regulations on mandatory deductions from salaries of government employees and remittance to the concerned agencies.
observations.managementAction: The CHRMO is reviewing outstanding balances with GSIS, Pag-IBIG, and PhilHealth to ensure accurate accounting and timely remittance. The City Accountant is reviewing records and will prepare a report for unremitted deductions and coordinate with HRMO. The City Treasurer mentioned employees who have not yet submitted their HDMF and PhilHealth numbers despite reminders.
observations.reason: Further verification and analysis are being conducted; initial findings indicate balances were transferred from the General Fund to the Trust Fund and the reconciliation process is ongoing.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:

a. Inventory Committee, the OIC – CGSO and the City Accountant to:
1. Properly plan and prepare the PIP to ensure an orderly conduct of the one-
time cleansing of PPE following the guidelines prescribed in COA Circular
2020-006 to account for all PPE, reconcile the property accounting records
and resolve the unreconciled discrepancy of P7,688,722,658.09 between the
total PPE per books and the total PPE in the submitted RPCPPE; and
2. Investigate the whereabouts of the PPE costing P13,133,799.39, which were
reported as not found or marked “missing” in the inventory report, and
identify the officials/employees accountable/responsible for the
missing/unaccounted property and collect the replacement cost from them
if no request for relief from property accountability was filed/granted.

b. Inventory Committee to:
3. Reconstitute the Disposal Committee to facilitate the disposal of the
properties totaling P83,819,067.81; and henceforth,
4. Prepare the separate RPCPPE for all the PPE accounts in the Statement of
Financial Position.

c. City Accountant to:
5. Prepare PPELC for all PPE items in compliance with Section 114 of MNGAS
for LGUs, Volume I.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the:

a. City Assessor to:
1. Conduct continuous ocular inspections at the barangay level to validate the
actual use of the real properties;
2. Prepare and submit to the City Treasurer, on or before December 31 of each
year, an assessment roll containing a list of all persons whose real
properties have been newly assessed or reassessed, along with the values
of such properties;

b. City Treasurer to update their records based on the assessment roll prepared
by the City Assessor, showing the names of taxpayers and the amount due and
collectible for the year, and furnish the City Accountant with the updated
records as basis for setting up the RPT/SET Receivable and the corresponding
Deferred RPT /SET Income at the beginning of the year;

c. City Accountant to:
1. Provide the necessary documentation for the adjustment of P43,725,813.50,
such as a detailed schedule or list of items that were reconciled in the RPT
and SET Receivables to outline the transactions that were corrected and
provide transparency regarding the adjustment;
2. Perform a comprehensive reconciliation of the RPT and SET Receivables,
including a review of all the transactions for CYs 2023 and 2024 and
thereon, promptly address and correct any discrepancies between the
General Ledger, the RPT Account Register, and other relevant records;
3. Coordinate with the City Treasurer to conduct a thorough reconciliation of
the RPT and SET Receivables as reflected in the financial statements with
the Certified List of RPT Delinquencies and document all adjustments
supported with detailed schedules; and
4. Prepare a JEV setting up the RPT and SET Receivables at the beginning of
the year based on the updated and certified list of RPT Account
Register/Taxpayer’s index card.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to:

a. Immediately secure or reconstruct the necessary documents for all dormant
accounts, retrieve lost or missing records and gather relevant information from
responsible officers; otherwise
b. Strictly adhere to the procedures outlined in COA Circular No. 2023-008, such as
review of the existence and validity of these dormant accounts, and prepare a
request for its derecognition of these dormant accounts from the books of
accounts and submit the same to the Audit Team for approval.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to:

a. Determine the validity of the outstanding obligations totaling P20,822,017.85,
which have remained outstanding for more than two to seven years, and, if
there are no legitimate claims against the City, revert the amount to the
unappropriated surplus; and henceforth,
b. Strictly comply with Section 98 of PD No. 1445 regarding the reversion of long-
outstanding payables.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:

a. City Accountant to:
1. Prepare and submit the complete BRS as of December 31, 2024;
2. Identify the details and nature of the reconciling items;
3. Make the necessary adjustments to the account;
4. Verify the nature of the unrecorded reconciling items amounting to
P3,322,383.14 before making the required adjustments to the books; and
b. City Accountant and City Treasurer to trace the discrepancy of P57,398,842.72
between the accounting records and Cashbook balances and immediately
effect the necessary adjustments.
Henceforth, it is recommended that the City Mayor require the:

a. City Accountant to submit the duplicate and quadruplicate copies of the
monthly BRS including the paid checks, and original copies of debit/credit
memos to the Audit Team for verification within 10 days of receipt after the end
of each month, as prescribed under Sections 3.2 to 3.4 of COA Circular No. 96-
011; and
b. City Accountant and City Treasurer to conduct regular reconciliation of
balances to ensure that any adjustments made or errors detected by either
office are appropriately reflected in their respective records, thereby ensuring
the accuracy of the reported CIB-LCCA balance.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:

a. City Accountant to submit the complete SLs and schedules for the Advances
to Officers and Employees and Advances for Operating Expenses accounts;
b. City Treasurer to present the cashbook for AOs who were granted with CAs, for
a comprehensive evaluation by the Audit Team;
c. City Accountant and City Treasurer to discontinue the practice of granting
additional or new CAs to AOs with outstanding CAs unless their previous
advances have been fully liquidated;
d. City Accountant and the City Treasurer to propose practical and effective
measures to address delays in liquidation, such as but is not limited to,
designating additional AOs to manage the City’s CAs, sending demand letters
to concerned officers and employees who have not liquidated their CAs within
the prescribed period, with a reminder that their salaries may be withheld if they
fail to liquidate after the demand; and henceforth
e. Strict enforcement of compliance with the granting and utilization of all cash
advances in accordance with PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 97-002.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to:

a. Prioritize the preparation and maintenance of SLs for both the Due from Other
Funds and Due to Other Funds accounts and ensure that they accurately reflect
all transactions;
b. Reconcile the balances of the Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds
accounts, ensuring that each entry in the Due from account corresponds to an
entry in the Due to account and vice versa; and
c. Strictly adhere to COA Circular No. 2015-009 to ensure that the balances of the
Due from and Due to Other Funds accounts are in agreement and compliance
with established accounting standards.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:

a. City Budget Officer to ensure that expenses are properly budgeted in their
respective expense accounts. If specific expense accounts do not have
sufficient budget allocation, revise the budget allocation or seek approval
for additional funding through the Sangguniang Panlungsod, to avoid the
issuance of Notice of Disallowance; and
b. City Accountant to review the nature of each disbursement of the City and
identify the specific expense accounts to which they should be attributed
to accurately reflect the City's financial transactions and ensure
compliance with COA regulations.

We also recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to disclose
the required financial information in the NFS in the ensuing year, ensuring
compliance with Paragraphs 127 and 129 of IPSAS 1 to assist users in
understanding the FS.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to:

a. Prepare and submit the SLs and supporting schedules to the Audit Team to
facilitate a comprehensive review of the accounts; and
b. Provide detailed descriptions for all items listed under 'Other' in the NFS,
including the breakdown of individual components, to ensure transparency
and accuracy in financial reporting.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:

a. Inventory Committee to report the physical count of semi-expendable property
owned by the agency, by type, and still in the custody of the City General
Services Office using the RPCSP;
b. Property and/or Supply Custodian to maintain the RegSPI by fund for each
class of issued semi-expendable property to record promptly the issue, return,
reissue, disposal, and other information about the property if returned and the
description/information about the asset;
c. Inventory Committee, the OIC – General Services Officer (GSO), and the City
Accountant to prepare and maintain all required forms, registry, and reports
following the prescribed format to strengthen control over the semi-expendable
properties; and
d. City Accountant to use the newly introduced accounts to record the
purchase/acquisition and subsequent issuance of semi-expendable properties
for fair presentation of the financial statements.

We recommended that the City Mayor:

a. Direct the OIC-GSO to prepare and submit the consolidated PIF, which should
include details such as the latest appraised values/valuations, insurable
amounts, and corresponding premiums for all insurable properties and other
assets of the City, as required by COA Circular No. 2018-002; and
b. Require the OIC–GSO to process the application for insurance of all insurable
assets and properties of the City with the GSIS.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Treasurer and City
Accountant to:

a. Reconcile the discrepancies between the BIR Forms and the GL records to
identify and correct any errors or omissions in the recording of payments
made to the BIR;
b. Investigate the unremitted balance of P15,567,256.75 and address any
possible reasons for its non-remittance, whether it be due to clerical errors,
oversight, or other factors;
c. Take immediate corrective action for the remittance of P15,567,256.75 taxes
withheld from prior periods to avoid the imposition of the 25 percent penalty,
which could amount to P3,891,814.19, and to coordinate with the BIR to
resolve any outstanding issues to minimize the financial impact; and
henceforth,
d. Ensure the full and timely remittance of taxes withheld from employee
compensation and payments to suppliers in compliance with the provisions
of BIR Revenue Regulations No. 2-98 to avoid any penalties, surcharges, or
interest.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:

a. City Accountant and the City Human Resource Management Officer
(CHRMO) to validate the unremitted contributions under the Due to GSIS,
Due to Pag-IBIG, and Due to PhilHealth accounts to establish accurate
balances and immediately remit the amounts payable; and
b. City Treasurer and the City Accountant to strictly comply with the GSIS,
Pag-IBIG, and PhilHealth regulations on the remittance of withheld
contributions to avoid the risk of potential penalties and legal consequences.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Planning and Development
Coordinator and/or the City Engineer to:

a. Ensure that all future infrastructure projects undergo thorough and detailed
engineering reviews, including comprehensive site surveys, land
ownership verification, and coordination with relevant stakeholders to avoid
overlapping construction activities and ensure all legal and regulatory
requirements are met before project initiation; and henceforth,
b. Enforce strict adherence to the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 and Section
3.2.3 of DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1, dated November 4, 2020, to ensure that
all projects are fully procurement-ready before implementation, thereby
avoiding delays in completion.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Disaster Risk Reduction
and Management Officer (CDRRMO), in coordination with the CDRRMC, to:

a. Carefully plan and identify PPAs that are well-prepared for procurement and
implementation, chargeable against LDRRMF, to avoid delays and non-
implementation; and
b. Cause the optimal utilization of the LDRRMF to enhance the disaster
preparedness and response capabilities of the community and lessen the
socio-economic and environmental impacts of disasters.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the: a. City Accountant to draw JEV to adjust the amount of stale checks in the books of accounts and restore related payable accounts; b. City Treasurer to monitor the checks that were not yet encashed in the depository bank and notify the payees of the existence of the checks to avoid them becoming stale; and c. Strictly comply with Section 59, Chapter 3 of MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I.
observations.managementAction: The City Accountant drew JEV Nos. 100-2024-03-067 and 200-2024-03-038, both dated March 31, 2024, to adjust the amount of stale checks in the books. The City Treasurer monitored the checks that had not yet been encashed in the depository bank and notified the payees. The concerned offices strictly complied.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor: a. Maximize the utilization of the 20 percent DF budget to help achieve desirable socio-economic and environmental development outcomes of the City; b. Strictly comply with the provisions of Section 3.2.3 of the DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1.
observations.managementAction: Management is working to maximize the utilization of the 20 percent DF by implementing projects such as the rehabilitation of drainage canals, installation of street lights, and the supply and installation of a WIFI network for the City, with a total payment of P112,698,195.98 under the current and continuing funds. These projects are ongoing. The City Engineering Office has provided updates: one project is pending due to land ownership issues; the other resumed on January 6th and is expected to be completed by May 15, 2025. The office will implement measures to address identified deficiencies and ensure future projects are procurement-ready.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the GAD Focal Person to: a. Ensure that all the PPAs included in the GAD Plan and Budget (GPB) will be implemented during the year so that the envisioned GAD objectives and results will be fully achieved; and b. Strictly comply with the provisions of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2016-01.
observations.managementAction: Based on the GAD Accomplishment Report, the City utilized P134,441,317.16 or 91.49 percent of its total GAD appropriation of P146,947,870.20 for CY 2024. Management confirmed implementation of the GPB during the year and observed the provisions pertaining to appropriation and utilization of GAD Fund.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the OIC - City Engineer to: a) institute proper planning in the preparation of engineering and program of works before awarding of the contract and b) strictly monitor the implementation of the projects to avoid delay that will deprive the constituents of timely receipt of the benefits that could be derived therefrom.
observations.managementAction: No action was taken by the Management. Implementation of the projects was not strictly monitored to avoid delay in completion; projects need resolution of some problems.
observations.reason: As of December 31, 2023, the three projects were not yet completed due to some problems that needs to be resolved.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor:

a) instruct the City Accountant to prepare the list of PPE items per PPELC and
compare it with the RPCPPE of the GSO;

b) direct the Property Unit to prepare a list of PPE items which are recorded in
the PPELCs but not included in the RPCPPE/Physical Inventory Report as
well as PPE items which are included in the RPCPPE but not recorded in the
PPELCs;

c) instruct the City Accountant and the Property Unit to submit both lists and
the latest RPCPPE/Physical Inventory Report to the Inventory Committee.
These documents shall be considered as inventory working papers and shall
be used by the Inventory Committee as basis in preparing the Property
Inventory Plan (PIP); and

d) direct the Inventory Committee to proceed with the preliminary activities of
One-Time Cleansing.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to:

a) draw a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to recognize the interest income
earned by debiting Cash in Bank - Local Currency, Time Deposits and
crediting Prior Period Adjustment by P1,854,783.37; and

b) present Cash in Bank - Local Currency, Time Deposits with maturity date of
360 days as Investments in the Financial Statements in accordance with
Paragraph 9 of IPSAS 2.

We recommended that the City Mayor:

a) require the AO to immediately settle her unliquidated cash advances.
Henceforth, compel all employees granted with cash advance to liquidate
the same as soon as the purposes for which these were given have been
served;

b) instruct the City Accountant that subsequent grant of cash advance shall
be supported with certification that their previous cash advances were
already liquidated and or proper accounting thereof is made;

c) instruct the concerned personnel to combine cash advances of less than
P20,000.00 especially if the purpose of the cash advances is the same;

d) if possible, lessen the bulk of cash advances granted to the AO and
transfer them to special disbursing officers with less transactions for her to
be able to liquidate her cash advances on time and update her cashbooks;
and

e) install, implement and monitor a sound system of internal control to
address risks and to provide reasonable assurance the operation of the
agency is executed orderly, ethically, economically, efficiently and
effectively, applicable laws are complied with and resources are
safeguarded from loss, misuse and damage.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:

a) City Accountant to draw Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to adjust the amount
of stale checks in the books of accounts and restore related payable
accounts;

b) City Treasurer to monitor the checks that were not yet encashed in the
depository bank and notify the payees of the existence of the checks to
avoid them becoming stale; and

c) henceforth, strictly comply with Section 59, Chapter 3 of MNGAS for LGUs,
Volume I.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to:

a) verify the validity of payables aged two years and more and send
confirmation letters for balances with identified claimants;

b) otherwise, revert to the unappropriated surplus the accounts payables of
more than two years whose claims are no longer valid; and

c) henceforth, strictly comply with Section 98 of PD No. 1445 on the
reversion of long outstanding payables.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to reconcile
the difference between the accounts RPT Receivables and SET Receivables
and make the necessary adjustments, furnishing the Office of the Auditor with
a copy of the Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) taking into account the
adjustments made.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Treasurer to advise her
disbursing officers to update the cashbook regularly and record liquidations
and refund chronologically and on a timely manner, in compliance with the
provisions of Section 28 of MNGAS for LGUs, Volume II and COA Circular
No. 97-002.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to prepare
and maintain SLs for each disbursing officer pursuant to Section 10, Chapter
12 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume II.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:

• City Treasurer to instruct the AOs to liquidate the cash advances within the
prescribed period and refund unused cash advances within two months; and
• City Accountant to deny the request for additional cash advance if the
Accountable/Disbursing Officer (AO/DO) has an existing unliquidated cash
advances pursuant to 4.1.2 of COA Circular No. 97-022.

We recommended that the City Mayor:

a) direct the OIC-City GSO to process the application for insurance of all
insurable assets and properties of the City with the GSIS following the
guidelines prescribed under COA Circular No. 2018-002;

b) instruct the OIC-City Budget Officer to include in the City’s annual budget
the appropriation for the payment of insurance premiums of all insurable
assets and properties of the City; and

c) direct the City Accountant and City Treasurer to provide necessary
assistance to the OIC-City GSO to ensure that all insurable assets and
properties of the City will be covered with insurance in compliance with RA
No. 656 and COA Circular No. 2018-002.

We reiterated our recommendation that the City Mayor instruct the CPDC to:

• maximize the utilization of the 20 percent Development Fund budget to help
achieve desirable socio-economic development and environmental
outcomes of the City; and

• henceforth, strictly comply with the provisions of Section 3.2.3 of the DBM-DOF
-DILG JMC No. 2020-1.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Disaster Risk Reduction
Management Officer (CDRRMO) to:

a) adequately plan and monitor the PPAs listed in the LDRRMFIP for effective
utilization of LDRRMF; and

b) henceforth strictly comply with the provisions of Section 21 of RA No. 10121
and NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-1.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the GAD Focal Person to:

• ensure that all the PPAs included in the GAD Plan and Budget (GPB) will be
implemented during the year so that the envisioned GAD objectives and
results will be fully achieved; and

• henceforth, strictly comply with the provisions of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA
JMC No. 2016-01.

We recommended that the City Mayor:
a) Instruct the City Accountant and the Officer in Charge- General Services Office (OIC-GSO) to maintain and update PPELCs and PCs, respectively, to facilitate reconciliation of the PPE accounts; b) Adopt the guidelines and procedures for one-time cleansing of PPE account balances as provided by COA Circular No. 2020-006; and c) Henceforth, strictly observe compliance with the provisions of MNGAS, Volume I for LGUs and COA Circular No. 2020-006.
observations.managementAction: PPELCs and PCs were updated and reconciliation of PPE accounts is on-going.
observations.reason: Reconciliation of PPELC and RPCPPE is on-going.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant and the City Treasurer to:
a) reconcile the difference between the Subsidiary Ledger and Cashbook balances of LBP Account No. 3262-1000-48; b) effect the necessary adjustments in the Subsidiary Ledger and Cashbook, if warranted, to adjust and arrive at the correct Cash in Bank-Local Currency balance; and c) strictly comply with the provisions of Section 10 of the MNGAS for LGUs, and Item 6.3 of COA Circular No. 97-022.
observations.managementAction: Subsidiary Ledger and Cashbook balances of LBP Account No. 3262-1000-48 were reconciled.
observations.reason: The negative balance of LBP Account No. 3262-1000-48 was automatically adjusted upon receipt of the Internal Revenue Allotment on January 2024.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendations that the City Mayor:
a) require all personnel with outstanding cash advances to immediately settle their unliquidated cash advances; b) instruct the City Accountant that subsequent grant of cash advance shall be supported with certification that their previous cash advances were already liquidated and or proper accounting thereof is made; and c) impose the withholding of salary of accountable officers who failed to effect the prompt liquidation of their cash advances.
observations.managementAction: Not all personnel with outstanding cash advances were able to settle their unliquidated cash advances. The Accounting Office reported that out of the unliquidated cash advances of the AO as of December 31, 2023, only P8,065,225.69 or 26.97 percent remained unliquidated as of March 31, 3024. The Accounting Office explained that it is very vital for the City to not hamper the grant of additional cash advances especially if it is for social services. The Local Financial Committee explained that since the subject AO is the only regular disbursing officer, the three others are special disbursing officers, regular cash advances were issued to her but they promised that they will think of other ways to lessen the cash advances granted to Ms. Dedicatoria for her to be able to liquidate her cash advances on time.
observations.reason: Most of the unliquidated cash advances belonged to Ms. Dedicatoria to whom the big volume of cash advances was given. For preparation of Recommendations Tracking Sheet (RTS) for the above recommendations to be considered closed for having been reiterated with modifications.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant and OIC-City General Services Officer to: a) Disclose the required financial information in the Notes to Financial Statements in the ensuing year to assist the users in understanding the financial statements; b) Conduct inventory of the Local Road Network and prepare and submit the RLRN and RPCLRN yearly; and c) Henceforth, strictly comply with the prescribed format provided in COA Circular No. 2015-008.
observations.managementAction: The required financial information was disclosed in the Notes to Financial Statements. Inventory of Local Road Network and RLRN was conducted and RLRN and RPCLRN were prepared and submitted. The prescribed format provided in COA Circular No. 2015-008 was being followed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to: a) record the Expenses of unliquidated cash advances only upon submission of the liquidation report; b) liquidation reports submitted the following year shall be recorded as Prior Period’s Adjustment; and c) henceforth, strictly comply with the Local Government Chart of Accounts prescribed under COA Circular No. 2015-009.
observations.managementAction: Expenses of unliquidated cash advances were recorded upon submission of the liquidation report. Liquidation reports the following year were recorded as Prior Period’s Adjustment. The Local Government Chart of Accounts prescribed under COA Circular No. 2015-009 was complied.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to: a) draw a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to correct the erroneous journal entries; b) obtain a copy of Status Report on Government Programs/Projects/Activities (GPPAs) from Engineering Office, and basis for reconciliation and recording in the books of accounts; and c) henceforth, strictly comply with the provisions of Section 50 of the MNGAS, Volume I for LGUs and IPSAS 17.
observations.managementAction: A JEV No. 100-2023-01-016 dated January 31, 2023 was drawn to correct the erroneous journal entries. A copy of Status Report on GPPAs from Engineering Office was obtained for reconciliation and basis for recording in the books of accounts. Provisions of Section 50 of the MNGAS, Volume I for LGUs and IPSAS 17 were complied.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to: a) prepare and submit the Reconciliation of Statement of Comparison of Budget and Actual Amounts to Statement of Financial Performance; b) disclose the required financial information in the Notes to FS in the ensuing year to assist the users in understanding the FS; and c) henceforth, strictly comply with IPSAS 2 and Paragraphs 127 and 129 of IPSAS 1.
observations.managementAction: Reconciliation of Statement of Comparison of Budget and Actual Amounts to Statement of Financial Performance was prepared and submitted. The required financial information was disclosed in the Notes to FS. Paragraph 1 of IPSAS 2 and Paragraphs 127 and 129 of IPSAS 1 were complied.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Assessor to: a) see to it that revision of real property assessments be made every three years to update the fair market value of taxable properties and derive additional income therefrom; b) prioritize the completion of documents necessary to the revision process of the real property assessments as indorsed by the Bureau of Local Government Finance (BLGF) to assure its effectivity on CY 2024; and c) henceforth, strictly comply with the provisions of Section 219 of RA No. 7160.
observations.managementAction: Revision of the real property assessments were made and returned documents were submitted to the BLGF. However, the new OIC-Regional Director of BLGF returned the revised documents and some schedules were re-evaluated.
observations.reason: Depends upon the issuance of Sangguniang Panlalawigan Resolution on the revision of real property tax assessments. The revised documents were returned by the new OIC-Regional Director of BLGF who totally disregarded the previous reviews.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the Human Resource Management Office (HRMO) to: a) prepare the Contract of Services, MOA and Job Order between the City and the individual; b) submit the Contract of Services, MOA and Job Order to the CSCRO for review; and c) strictly adhere with the provisions of CSC Resolution No. 02-0790 regarding policy guidelines for Contract of Services, MOA and Job Order.
observations.managementAction: Contract of Services, MOA and Job Order between the City of Cabuyao and the individual were prepared and submitted to the CSCRO for review. Provisions of CSC Resolution No. 02-0790 regarding policy guidelines for Contract of Services, MOA and Job Order were complied.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to: a) prepare the Notice to Proceed in the succeeding procurement of goods and include it in the contract profile submitted to the Audit Team; and b) strictly adhere with the provisions of Section 37.4.1 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.
observations.managementAction: Notice to Proceed was prepared in succeeding procurement and was included in the contract profile submitted to the Audit Team. Compliance with Section 37.4.1 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 was observed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the (BAC) Secretariat and City General Services Office (CGSO) to: a) explain in writing the reason/s as to incurring the delay in the submission of copies of purchase orders together with the required supporting documents; b) create a detailed plan or policy to ensure prompt submission of the copies of purchase orders; and c) henceforth, ensure strict compliance with the provisions in COA Circular Nos. 2009-001 and 2009-002.
observations.managementAction: The Management had explained the reason/s of delay in the submission of copies of purchase orders together with the required supporting documents. A strategy was developed to ensure prompt submission of copies of POs. Compliance with COA Circular Nos. 2009-001 and 2009-002 was observed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the CPDO and the City Engineer to: a) address the cause/s for the delay and/or non-implementation of the DF projects; b) maximize the utilization of the 20 percent DF budget to help achieve desirable socio-economic development and environmental outcomes of the City; and c) henceforth, strictly comply with the provisions of Section 5 of the DILG and DBM JMC No. 2017-1.
observations.managementAction: No action was taken by the Management. Delay in the implementation of projects was due to election ban period which coincided with the implementation stages of opening, construction and rehabilitation of road networks and other related projects. The CPDO committed to expedite the process for the remaining projects to ensure full utilization by 2024.
observations.reason: Delay in the implementation of projects was due to election ban period which coincided with the implementation stages of opening, construction and rehabilitation of road networks and other related projects.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Disaster Risk Reduction Management Office (CDRRMO) to: a) adequately plan and monitor the PPAs listed in the LDRRMIP for effective utilization of LDRRMF; and b) henceforth strictly comply with the provisions of Section 21 of RA No. 10121 and NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-1.
observations.managementAction: No action was taken by the Management. The CDRRMO explained that under-spending was due to no major disaster or calamity and that some projects’ programs of work were just prepared. Administrative Officer noted that infrastructure projects could be constructed by phase.
observations.reason: Under-spending due to no major disaster or calamity and issues in project readiness; some PPAs not adequately planned.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the GAD Focal Person to ensure implementation of GPB PPAs and to strictly comply with PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2016-01.
observations.managementAction: The City Planning and Development Council Office explained that they are conducting workshops to equip department heads and personnel to develop, implement and monitor GAD-related projects effectively and are strengthening monitoring and evaluation mechanism.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to disclose the required financial information in the Notes to Financial Statements in the ensuing year to assist the users in understanding the financial statements and to provide them with additional information value on the accounts.
observations.managementAction: The City Accountant requested to revise the Notes to Financial Statements to include the disclosure and said they will submit the updated Notes to Financial Statements on May 6, 2022; however they were not able to comply due to lack of time and manpower.
observations.reason: Lack of time and manpower prevented timely revision and submission of updated Notes.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendations that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to strictly enforce and observe the timely submission of the monthly trial balances and financial statements, as prescribed under Sections 70 and 72 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I; and impose the withholding of salary of officers and employees who failed to effect the prompt submission of monthly TBs and financial statements and other reports.
observations.managementAction: No action taken by Management to impose withholding of salaries; City Accountant requested cooperation of Department Heads to comply with cut-off of processing of transactions and cited manual encoding and lack of manpower as reasons for delay. Preparation of monthly TBs started only after submission of year-end TBs which were late.
observations.reason: Delay caused by manual processing, voluminous transactions, lack of manpower and disruptions due to COVID-19 pandemic.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendations that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant and OIC-City General Services Officer to maintain special accounts for Market, Slaughterhouse, Cemetery, Hospitals and BGCM releases to Management in compliance with Section 313 of RA No. 7160, Chapter VI of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I, and Section 3.7 of LBC No. 125.
observations.managementAction: No action was taken by Management at the time; City Accountant reasoned that market, slaughterhouse and others were not considered as economic enterprises in their view. There was an appropriation for enterprises but no formal maintenance of those special accounts in GF books.
observations.reason: Inadequate information and differing interpretation whether certain operations constitute economic enterprises; accounting treatment not yet aligned.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the City Mayor instruct the City Social Welfare Officer to fully implement the PPAs in coordination with other City officials for the benefits and protection of children, and to specify the programs included in the LCPC budget in compliance with Section 15 of RA No. 9344 and DILG MC No. 2012-120.
observations.managementAction: No action was taken by Management to provide more detailed PPAs; implementation of LCPC activities was limited.
observations.reason: Lack of manpower and pandemic affected planning and implementation; PPAs were not specified.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management:
a) conduct actual physical inventory of the City’s PPE to establish its
existence;
b) prepare and maintain Property Cards to facilitate reconciliation with the
accounting records; and
c) henceforth, strictly observe compliance with Sections 119 and 124 of
the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I.

We reiterated our recommendations that the City Mayor direct the City
Accountant and City Treasurer to:
a) reconcile the difference between the Subsidiary Ledgers and
Cashbooks balances of Cash in Bank- Local Currency Current
Account;
b) effect the necessary adjustments in the Subsidiary Ledgers and
Cashbooks, where applicable, to adjust and arrive at the correct Cash
in Bank- Local Currency Current balances; and
c) strictly comply with the provisions of Section 10 of the MNGAS for
LGUs, Volume II and Item 6.3 of Commission on Audit (COA)
Circular No. 97-022.

We reiterated our recommendations that the City Mayor direct the City
Accountant and OIC-City General Services Officer to:
a. disclose the required financial information in the Notes to Financial
Statements in the ensuing year to assist the users in understanding the
financial statements; and
b. conduct inventory of the Local Road Network and prepare and submit
the RLRN and RPCLRN yearly; and
c. henceforth, strictly comply with the prescribed format provided in COA
Circular No. 2015-008.

We reiterated our recommendations that the City Mayor instruct the:
a) City Treasurer to submit to the City Accountant at the beginning of the
year the duly certified list showing the name of the taxpayers and the
amount due and collectible for the year as basis for the setting-up of
Real Property Tax (RPT) Receivables and Special Education Tax (SET)
Receivables and the corresponding contra accounts Deferred Real
Property Tax and Deferred Special Education Tax;
b) City Accountant to record advance payments of RPT/SET Receivables
as Other Deferred Credits; and
c) City Accountant to assign somebody to reconcile the difference
between the accounts Real Property Tax Receivables and Special
Education Tax Receivables. Review the entries made specifically the
setting-up of Real Property Tax Receivables and Special Education
Tax Receivables and make the necessary adjustments, furnishing the
Office of the Auditor with a copy of the Journal Entry Voucher (JEV)
taking into account the adjustments made.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to:
a) verify the validity of payables aged two years and more and send
confirmation letters for balances with identified claimants;
b) otherwise, revert to the unappropriated surplus the accounts payables of
more than two years whose claims are no longer valid;
c) review and monitor the balances of the remaining accounts payable to
determine those claims that are no longer valid; and
d) henceforth, strictly comply with Section 98 of PD No. 1445 on reversion
of long outstanding payables.

We reiterated our recommendations that the Management instruct the City
Accountant to:
a) make sure that the entries in Due from Other Funds accounts have
corresponding entries in the Due to Other Funds account and vice versa;
and
b) reconcile the difference in the reciprocal accounts Due from Other
Funds and Due to Other Funds and make the necessary adjustments to
ensure that the balances of both accounts are in agreement.

We reiterated our recommendations that the City Mayor:
a) require all personnel with outstanding cash advances to immediately
settle their unliquidated cash advances. Henceforth, compel all
employees granted with cash advance to liquidate the same as soon as
the purposes for which these were given have been served;
b) instruct the City Accountant that subsequent grant of cash advance is
supported with certification that their previous cash advance was
already liquidated and/or proper accounting thereof is made;
c) impose the withholding of salary of accountable officers who failed to
effect the prompt liquidation of their cash advances; and
d) henceforth, strictly comply with COA Circular No. 97-002 on the grant
and liquidation of cash advances.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to:
a) prepare and submit the BRS within the reglementary period and prepare
the necessary adjusting entries for any reconciling items identified;
b) close the books of accounts as of year-end to establish the year-end
balances of the cash accounts and the preparation of BRS; and
c) henceforth, strictly comply with the provisions of Items 3.2 to 3.4 of COA
Circular No. 96-011 and Section 4.1 of COA Circular No. 2010-001.

We recommended that the City Mayor:
a) Require the MSW to reconcile her reports with the Accounting Office;
b) Require the certification of the Chief Accountant in the monthly reports
and liquidation reports submitted to DOH-CHD; and
c) Henceforth, strictly observe compliance with Items 2.3 and 2.4 of the
MOA.

We reiterated our recommendations that the City Mayor direct the City
Accountant to:
a) disclose the required financial information in the Notes to Financial
Statements in the ensuing year to assist the users in understanding the
financial statements;
b) provide particulars in recording to the subsidiary ledgers for proper
identification of the details of the transactions;
c) henceforth, strictly comply with Paragraph 1 of IPSAS 2 and Paragraphs
127 and 129 of IPSAS 1.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to:
a) observe the regular and timely submission of the financial statements
and other related reports;
b) request for an accounting/computer system that will generate the the
financial statements and other related reports; and
c) strictly comply with the provisions of Section 122 of PD No. 1445,
Section 7.2.1(a) of the RRSA prescribed by COA Circular No. 2009-006
and Sections 70 and 72 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I.

We reiterated our recommendations that the City Mayor direct the OIC-City General Services Officer to: • coordinate with the Accounting Office to reconcile their property records and to ascertain the cause of the total discrepancy between the Accounting records and the Report on the Physical Count of Inventory Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE); • ensure the conduct of a complete annual physical count of PPE, submit the duly approved RPCPPE within the prescribed period and see to it that the RPCPPE is reconciled with the accounting records; and; • assign Property Number to each PPE item which will be useful for identification purposes.
observations.managementAction: Inventory of PPE accounts was started but reconciliation delays the completion of the task. Actual inventory of prior years’ PPE was not yet conducted. Each PPE was assigned Property Number starting 2020.
observations.reason: The volume of PPE accounts delays the reconciliation and completion of the task.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendations that the City Mayor instruct the: a) City Accountant and the City Treasurer to conduct periodic reconciliation of the cash accounts as of a given date, in compliance with Section 63(F) of PD No. 1445; and b) City Accountant to prepare and submit the BRS within the reglementary period, and prepare the necessary adjusting entries for any reconciling items identified in the process, in accordance with Items 3.2 to 3.4 of COA Circular No. 96-011 dated October 2, 1996, and Section 7.2.1 of the RRSA prescribed under COA Circular No. 2009-006, thus resulted in unreliable and doubtful balances of the cash accounts at year-end.
observations.managementAction: Reconciliation of cash accounts is ongoing for the current year. BRS were not submitted within the reglementary period but JEV No. 2021-12-214 was prepared to adjust reconciling items. Some reconciliation work was done; voluminous transactions take time to analyze and compare with the bank statements.
observations.reason: Voluminous transactions that take time to process/reconcile and delays in submission of supporting documents; late closing of books.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendations that the City Mayor: a) require all personnel with outstanding cash advances to immediately settle their unliquidated cash advances. Henceforth, compel all employees granted with cash advance to liquidate the same as soon as the purposes for which these were given have been served; b) instruct the City Accountant that subsequent grant of cash advance is supported with certification that their previous cash advance was already liquidated and/or proper accounting thereof is made; c) instruct the City Accountant to use the appropriate account for cash advances granted for official travel and operating expenses and make the necessary adjustment/s; d) require the concerned Accountable Officers to liquidate and refund the balance of cash advances totaling P216,164.07; and e) impose the withholding of salary of accountable officers who failed to effect the prompt liquidation of their cash advances.
observations.managementAction: Some cash advances were settled in CY 2021. Refunds were made for certain items. City Accountant to ensure additional grants of cash advances shall be made only upon submission of liquidation reports. Balance of cash advances were liquidated in CY 2022 and balances were refunded.
observations.reason: Funds received from national government that need to be paid out within prescribed period hampered liquidation; pandemic constraints and need to disburse assistance affected timely liquidation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to draw JEV to adjust the amount of stale checks in the books of accounts following the procedures stated under Section 59, Chapter 3 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I. We also recommended monitoring of released checks that were not yet encashed in the depository bank and notify the payees of the existence of the checks to avoid/prevent them from becoming stale.
observations.managementAction: Stale checks were adjusted thru JEV No. 2021-12-214. Released checks that were not yet encashed in the depository banks were monitored and posted in the bulletin board.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant and OIC-City General Services Officer to: a. disclose the required financial information in the Notes to Financial Statements in the ensuing year to assist the users in understanding the financial statements; b. prepare and submit the RLRN and RPCLRN yearly in accordance with the prescribed format provided in COA Circular No. 2015-008; and c. strictly comply with the prescribed format provided in COA Circular No. 2015-008.
observations.managementAction: The City Accountant committed that the required disclosures will be included in the subsequent year’s financial statements. The GSO started the preparation of required reports.
observations.reason: Inadequate information on road networks and format issues; time constraint and late submission caused non-submission.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Treasurer to submit to the City Accountant at the beginning of the year the duly certified list showing the name of the taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year as basis for the setting-up of Real Property Tax (RPT) Receivables and Special Education Tax (SET) Receivables and the corresponding contra accounts Deferred Real Property Tax and Deferred Special Education Tax; the City Accountant to record advance payments of RPT/SET Receivables as Other Deferred Credits; and the City Accountant to reconcile the difference between the accounts Real Property Tax Receivables and Special Education Tax Receivables. Review the entries made specifically the setting-up of Real Property Tax Receivables and Special Education Tax Receivables and make the necessary adjustments, furnishing the Office of the Auditor with a copy of the Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) taking into account the adjustments made.
observations.managementAction: Analysis and reconciliation of the accounts were in process. City Accountant explained budget estimate was used due to non-provision of duly certified list by City Treasurer and the assessment roll by the City Assessor. City Accountant also explained RPT recorded at 70% only due to barangay share.
observations.reason: Reconciliation and analysis in process; differences accumulated over years and take time to reconcile.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to reconcile the difference in the reciprocal accounts Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds and make the necessary adjustments in the books of accounts to ensure that the balances of both accounts are in agreement.
observations.managementAction: Reconciliation of Due to and Due from Other Funds is currently being conducted. Per checking, most variances relate to Due to BIR which was paid through the General Fund.
observations.reason: Reconciliation in process; variances relate to items paid through different funds; accumulated differences from prior years take time to reconcile.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendations that the City Mayor require the: a) City Accountant to record purchases of supplies/goods and materials as inventory and adopt the Perpetual Inventory Method in accordance with Section 114 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I, and to recognize expenses upon issuance based on the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI); b) City Accountant and the OIC-City GSO to maintain updated Subsidiary Ledger Cards and Stock Cards, respectively, to account for the receipt and disposition of supplies and materials; and c) OIC-City GSO or the Department Heads to conduct and submit inventory of unused drugs and medicines and laboratory supplies and materials at year-end as basis for the recording of inventory accounts in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: Delivery of supplies and materials were received and inspected by the General Services Officer (GSO). Inventory of drugs and medicines and laboratory supplies was submitted. Updated Subsidiary Ledger Cards and Stock Cards are in process; maintenance of stockroom ongoing.
observations.reason: Time constraint and limitations brought by the pandemic and lack of manpower; stockroom records and subsidiary ledgers were not fully updated.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant and the City Treasurer to: a) immediately send notice to the concerned payees of unclaimed checks and submit thereafter the related DVs upon release of the checks; b) ensure that supporting documents and required signatories were complete before the preparation of checks; and c) observe timeliness in the submission of reports including DVs and their supporting documents to the Audit Team within ten days of the ensuing month in compliance with COA Circular Nos. 95-006 and 2009-006.
observations.managementAction: Some DVs totaling P83,482,531.13 were immediately submitted and related supporting documents were submitted to the Accounting Office for submission to COA. However, many DVs were still in the process of collection and submission; sorting of documents before submission to COA was implemented.
observations.reason: Some DVs were still in the process of retrieval and submission; late submission of reports and voluminous documents caused delays.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the OIC-City GSO to: a) prepare the IIRUP and initiate the disposal of unserviceable properties to prevent further deterioration, as required under Section 79 of PD No. 1445 and Section 125 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I; and b) submit explanation/justification on some PPEs which were declared as unserviceable but service thereof is not beyond their estimated useful life.
observations.managementAction: The GSO started preparation of the IIRUP. Justification/explanations were submitted to substantiate some PPEs which were declared unserviceable but service thereof is not beyond estimated useful life.
observations.reason: Time constraint and lack of manpower delayed full disposal process; preparation of IIRUP initiated.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the: a) City Accountant to submit DVs totaling P10,747,961.50 immediately and their supporting documents for verification and post-audit of the disbursements totaling P18,224,471.50; and to refrain from recognising as outright expense made directly to procured end-users upon receipt and follow/adopt the perpetual inventory method in recording and reporting purchases of inventory items as provided under Section 114 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I; b) General Services Officer to maintain the required reports for proper monitoring and reporting of receipts and issuances of inventories and coordinate with the City Accountant for the proper amount of inventory that will be recognized as expense for a period; and c) City DRRM Office or appropriate official to coordinate with the City Accountant for the submission of documents to substantiate the relief operations made by the City and for the Audit Team to determine whether the procured relief goods were completely distributed to the intended beneficiaries.
observations.managementAction: Some DVs were submitted to Accounting Office for submission to COA. The GSO maintained inventory reports and submitted inventories of drugs and medicines. Delivery of supplies to end-users was followed by inspection by the GSO and coordination with City Accountant was ongoing. Documentation to substantiate relief operations were submitted.
observations.reason: Some documents still pending; delivery and end-user issuance practices needed alignment with perpetual inventory method; coordination and documentation in process.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor: • delegate the receipt and acknowledgement of in-kind donations to the CGSO, being the property and supply unit, for proper and complete documentation and recording of the goods donated to the City; • require the Department/Office assigned in the distribution of the in-kind donations to coordinate with the City GSO for proper control and monitoring of the distributed and remaining items as of a given period; • require the City GSO, in coordination with the appropriate City Department/Office to prepare a Report/Summary/List of Donations Received, Distributed and Balances; and; • instruct the City Accountant to disclose in the Notes to the Financial Statements the relevant information regarding the receipt, issuance and balances of in-kind donations pursuant to COA Circular No. 2014-002.
observations.managementAction: No donations-in-kind were received for CY 2021. The City GSO and concerned offices were to be designated for receipt and acknowledgement when donations occur.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our prior recommendations that the City Mayor direct the City Development Council to: a) evaluate the necessity of the unimplemented projects, especially those in prior years, and reprogram the same to more important development projects that will cater to the present needs of the constituents; b) instruct the member-barangays at the planning stage to thoroughly determine the development projects that were needed by them before submitting to the council to avoid various reprogramming; and c) ensure that all projects funded out of the 20 percent DF will be implemented during the year in order to attain the desired socio-economic development and environmental outcomes.
observations.managementAction: For CY 2021, projects needed by barangays were determined; however, only 31.84% was utilized for CY 2021. The development projects needed by barangays were identified; reprogramming and pandemic affected implementation.
observations.reason: Low utilization due to pandemic and late closing of accounts; some projects reprogrammed; implementation constraints.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council to ensure the implementation of the programs, projects and activities under the LDRRMF in order to strengthen the City’s disaster preparedness and response capabilities and build a disaster-resilient community.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendations that the City Mayor direct the OIC-City
General Services Officer to:
 coordinate with the Accounting Office to reconcile their property records and
to ascertain the cause of the total discrepancy of P1,821,894,905.40;
 ensure the conduct of a complete annual physical count of PPE, submit the duly
approved RPCPPE within the prescribed period and see to it that the RPCPPE is
reconciled with the accounting records; and
 assign Property Number to each PPE item which will be useful for identification
purposes.

We reiterated our recommendations that the City Mayor instruct the:
a) City Accountant and the City Treasurer to conduct periodic reconciliation of
the cash accounts to ensure the completeness and accuracy of the recorded
transactions as of a given date, in compliance with Section 63(F) of the LTOM
and Section 74 of PD No. 1445; and
b) City Accountant to prepare and submit the BRS within the reglementary
period, and prepare the necessary adjusting entries for any reconciling items
identified in the process, in accordance with Items 3.2 to 3.4 of COA Circular
No. 96-011 dated October 2, 1996, and Section 7.2.1 of the RRSA prescribed
under COA Circular No. 2009-006.

We reiterated our recommendations that the City Mayor:
a) require all personnel with outstanding cash advances to immediately settle
their unliquidated cash advances. Henceforth, compel all employees granted
with cash advances to liquidate the same as soon as the purposes for which
these were given have been served;
b) instruct the City Accountant that subsequent grant of cash advance is
supported with certification that their previous cash advance was already
liquidated and or proper accounting thereof is made;
c) instruct the City Accountant to use the appropriate account for cash
advances granted for official travel and operating expenses and make the
necessary adjustment/s;
d) require the concerned Accountable Officers to liquidate and refund the
unexpended balance of cash advances totaling P216,164.07; and
e) impose the withholding of salary of accountable officers who failed to effect
the prompt liquidation of their cash advances.

We reiterated our recommendation that the City Mayor require the City
Accountant to draw Journal Entry Voucher to adjust the amount of stale checks
in the books of accounts following the procedures stated under Section 59,
Chapter 3 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I. We also recommended the
monitoring of released checks that were not yet encashed in the depository bank
and notify the payees of the existence of the checks to avoid/prevent them from
becoming stale.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant and OIC-City
General Services Officer to:
a. disclose the required financial information in the Notes to Financial
Statements in the ensuing year to assist the users in understanding the
financial statements; and
b. prepare and submit the RLRN and RPCLRN yearly in accordance with the
prescribed format provided in COA Circular No. 2015-008.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to reconcile
the difference between the accounts Real Property Tax Receivables and Special
Education Tax Receivables. Review and analyze the entries made specifically the
setting-up of Real Property Tax Receivables and Special Education Tax
Receivables and make the necessary adjustments, furnishing the Office of the
Auditor with a copy of the Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) taking into account the
adjustments made.

We reiterated our recommendation that the City Mayor instruct the City
Accountant to reconcile the difference in the reciprocal accounts Due from Other
Funds and Due to Other Funds and make the necessary adjustments in the books
of accounts to ensure that the balances of both accounts are in agreement.

We reiterated our recommendations that the City Mayor require the:
a) City Accountant to record purchases of goods/supplies and materials as
inventory adopting the Perpetual Inventory Method in accordance with
Section 114 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I, and to recognize expenses
upon their issuance based on the SSMI;
b) City Accountant and the OIC-City General Services Officer (GSO) to maintain
updated Subsidiary Ledger Cards and Stock Cards, respectively, to account
for the receipt and disposition of supplies and materials; and
c) OIC-City GSO or the Department Heads to conduct and submit inventory of
unused supplies and materials at year-end as basis for the recording of
inventory accounts in the financial statements.

We reiterated our recommendation that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant
to disclose the required financial information in the Notes to Financial
Statements in the ensuing year to assist the users in understanding the financial
statements, and to provide them with additional information value on the
accounts.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant and City
Treasurer to:
a. immediately send notice to the concerned payees of unclaimed checks and
submit thereafter the related DVs upon release of the checks;
b. ensure that supporting documents and required signatories were complete
before the preparation of checks; and
c. observe timeliness in the submission of reports including DVs and their
supporting documents to the Audit Team within ten days of the ensuing
month in compliance with COA Circular Nos. 95-006 and 2009-006.

We reiterated our recommendation that the City Mayor require the City
Accountant to strictly enforce and observe the timely submission of the monthly
trial balances and financial statements, as prescribed under Sections 70 and 72
of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I.

We also recommended that the City Mayor impose the withholding of salary of
officers and employees who failed to effect the prompt submission of monthly
trial balances and financial statements and other reports.

We reiterated our recommendation that the City Mayor instruct the City
Accountant to maintain special accounts for Market, Slaughterhouse, Cemetery,
Hospitals and BGCM releases to guide Management in making sound decisions
in their operations, in compliance with Section 313 of RA No. 7160, Chapter VI
of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I, and Section 3.7 of LBC No. 125.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the OIC-City General Services
Officer to:
a) prepare the IIRUP and initiate the disposal of unserviceable properties to
prevent further deterioration, as required under Section 79 of PD No. 1445 and
Section 125 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I; and
b) submit explanation/justification on some PPEs which were declared as
unserviceable but service thereof is not beyond their estimated useful life.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the:
a) City Accountant to:
 submit immediately the disbursement vouchers and their supporting
documents for the disbursements totaling P18,224,471.50 for verification
and post-audit by the Audit Team; and
 refrain from recognizing as outright expense procured inventory items,
instead follow/adopt the perpetual inventory method in recording and
reporting purchases of inventory items as provided under Section 114 of
the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I;
b) General Services Officer to maintain the required reports for proper
monitoring and reporting of the receipts and issuances of inventories and
coordinate with the City Accountant for the proper amount of inventory that
will be recognized as expense for a period; and

Accountant for the submission of the documents to substantiate the relief
operations made by the City and for the Audit Team to determine whether the
procured relief goods were completely distributed to the intended
beneficiaries.

We recommended that the City Mayor:
